entities.description: Letter X within Hingeston's chronologically arranged documentary edition. The exact unit occupies printed pp. 23-25: it starts at heading X in the lower half of p. 23 after the preceding letter and translation, and ends after the Middle French endorsement near the top of p. 25 before letter XI. Hingeston supplies the English heading, marginal summaries, manuscript/source note, cross-references, feast-date gloss, running date, and signatory note.
entities.label: Hingeston's 1860 edition of The Earl of March to Henry IV
notes: The chronological catalogue on printed p. xcviii explicitly dates this unit 18 February 1400.
The preface says source notes identify material and original/copy status and that missing text is supplied in brackets.
The p. 24 Rymer footnote reads viii. 131, not the XML's 181.

rights.basis: Hingeston died in 1910 according to provider metadata; the UK term for literary works is life plus 70 years, and published-edition typographical arrangement lasts 25 years. The 1860 edition is outside both terms.
rights.component: Hingeston's 1860 editorial text, arrangement, and typographical presentation

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00126.xml. The medieval letter, Hingeston editorial unit, U-M encoding, original f. 22 document, parent Cotton codex, digital facsimile, and catalog records are separate entities.

The intellectual work is one letter with a Middle French address endorsement. Hingeston's supplied title, marginal summaries, witness note, cross-references, date gloss, and earl-identification note are editorial apparatus.

Every complete-volume cache and the encoding-to-edition relation preserve the spatial boundary: heading X starts in the lower half of printed p. 23 after the preceding letter and translation; the endorsement ends near the top of p. 25 before letter XI begins.

The original document is BL Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22, not the codex as a whole. The current BL catalog explicitly enumerates it as item 18, calls it original, dates it 18 February 1400 at Dunbar, and places f. 22 within a paper run.

The document's exact written side or sides and present survival of Hingeston's reported seal traces were not verified because the current BL record has no digitised-content link and no public manuscript image was found.

The letter gives the eighteenth day of February but no year. Hingeston's chronological catalogue assigns 1400 after describing his chronological method; the current BL catalog independently gives the same full date.

The primary language is normalized as Older Scots (specifically Early Scots) from current DSL historical periodization. The letter calls its language Englishe; DSL explains that Scots was called Inglis before 1494. U-M and the XML retain a legacy Middle English classification.

Hingeston's footnote on printed p. 24 reads 'See Rymer, viii. 131.' The checked-in XML and current U-M full-text display read '181'; this is preserved as a source/encoding discrepancy, not silently corrected.

Hingeston calls George Dunbar the eleventh earl. A current University of Edinburgh authority record calls George Dunbar (1338-1420) the tenth Earl of Dunbar and March; the preferred name remains ordinal-free.
The current BL physical description reports 130 folios, while an inherited Latin scope statement says 121. Current foliation controls access planning; the discrepancy is retained.

The Internet Archive scan derives from a PIMS-University of Toronto copy, call number 3050475. That physical source copy is distinct from the abstract edition, the scan bytes, and the IA metadata catalog object, even though the schema has no dedicated print-copy entity type.
The pinned CME LICENSE uses Unlicense wording framed as software; its applicability to corpus XML, data, and markup is not assumed.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository representation of University of Michigan CME00126, an encoding of Hingeston's letter X, 'The Earl of March to Henry IV,' in Royal and Historical Letters volume 1 (1860), printed pp. 23-25. The source unit begins at heading X in the lower half of p. 23 after the preceding letter and translation, and ends after a Middle French endorsement near the top of p. 25 before letter XI. Hingeston transcribed the original paper letter at British Library Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22, written at Dunbar on 18 February 1400 by George Dunbar, Earl of March. The day and month are internal; the year is explicitly supplied by Hingeston's chronological catalogue and independently recorded in the current BL catalog. A complete 596-page Internet Archive PDF, complete OCR, IA metadata JSON, and exact BL catalog JSON are cached with fixity. No public digital facsimile of f. 22 or the complete codex was found.

catalogSummary.author: George Dunbar, Earl of March
catalogSummary.title: The Earl of March to Henry IV

catalogingSubject.label: The Earl of March to Henry IV
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the single letter written at Dunbar by George Dunbar, Earl of March, to Henry IV on 18 February 1400, including its Middle French address endorsement. CME00126 also represents F. C. Hingeston's supplied heading, marginal summaries, witness note, footnotes, and signature identification as a distinct editorial layer. It is not Hingeston's 1860 volume as a whole, Cotton MS Vespasian F VII as a codex, the physical paper document at f. 22, the University of Michigan encoding, the Internet Archive scan, or the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Letter from George Dunbar to Henry IV

locators.value: f. 22; exact written side or sides not publicly verified

contentParts.summary: Salutation, complaint over the Duke of Rothesay's broken marriage obligation, offer of political service, requests for an intermediary, safe-conduct, and aid, kinship explanation, blessing, dateline, and signature.

contentParts.label: Middle French address endorsement

locators.value: p. 25, immediately after the signature and before letter XI

contentParts.summary: Address to the most excellent, powerful, and noble prince, the king of England.

contentParts.label: Hingeston editorial apparatus

locators.value: pp. 23-25, interleaved headings, margins, and footnotes
two HEAD and eight NOTE elements

notes: The count comprises two HEAD plus eight NOTE elements.
Modern English editorial prose is not treated as an intellectual-work language facet.
Hingeston's earl ordinal and the Rymer reference discrepancy remain attributed editorial claims rather than silently normalized facts.
contentParts.summary: Supplied item number and title, witness and cross-reference note, four marginal summaries, safe-conduct and feast-date footnotes, and identification of the signatory.

assertion.notes: The letter's dateline gives the eighteenth day of February but no year. Hingeston's chronological catalogue explicitly assigns 18 February 1400 after weighing internal evidence, and the current BL catalog independently records Dunbar, Feb. 18, 1400.

dateStatements.display: 18 February 1400 (day and month explicit; year supplied by Hingeston's chronological catalogue and the current British Library catalog)

assertion.notes: Hingeston is the earliest publication established in this research, not a proof that no earlier printing exists.

extent.description: Twelve cataloged prose structural items: one numbered letter work, one address endorsement, two editorial HEAD elements, and eight editorial NOTE elements, all in one DIV1; the letter body contains seven main P elements plus a closer, and numbered page breaks map printed pp. 23-25.

formStatements.description: A prose diplomatic letter with a brief prose address endorsement and nineteenth-century prose editorial apparatus.

assertion.notes: Dunbar complains of a broken marriage agreement and requests Henry's aid and a safe-conduct while offering service.

More specifically Early Scots under DSL's periodization (up to about 1450). The letter itself calls its vernacular 'Englishe'; DSL explains that Scots was called Inglis before 1494. U-M and the legacy XML normalize the text as Middle English (enm), which is retained as representation history rather than preferred work-level language cataloging.

languageStatements.label: Older Scots
notes: The normalized work-level code is sco. Modern English headings and notes belong to Hingeston's editorial layer and are not promoted to a work-level language facet.
Henry IV is the recipient and a subject of the letter, not a creator responsibility under the available schema.
The date's day and month are internal; the year is a high-confidence supplied date supported by both Hingeston's explicit chronological catalogue and the current BL record.
The Middle French endorsement is part of the transmitted document, but its hand and personal wording responsibility are not identified by the inspected sources.
The modern editorial title and notes are represented as apparatus rather than extra intellectual-work language facets.

next_steps: Consult a current specialist peerage history or an authoritative national name authority that explicitly explains the numbering convention.
Keep the preferred name ordinal-free until the convention is documented.
notes: The conflict may reflect whether Dunbar and March successions are counted separately or an older genealogical convention; no resolution is inferred here.
openQuestions.question: Which ordinal convention should be used for George Dunbar in this record: Hingeston's eleventh Earl or the current University of Edinburgh authority's tenth Earl of Dunbar and March?

next_steps: Verify the cited Foedera passage directly.
If confirmed, correct the XML in a separately scoped encoding change while preserving this manifest's description of the current artifact.
notes: The current U-M full-text display also reads 181; the discrepancy is not silently repaired in metadata.
openQuestions.question: Should the CME XML footnote 'See Rymer, viii. 181' be corrected to the source edition's 'See Rymer, viii. 131'?

assertion.notes: The letter's dateline says it was written at the writer's castle of Dunbar.

The signature uses the French title; the current BL catalog identifies the sender as Geo. Dunbar, Earl of March. No inspected source attributes the address endorsement's hand or wording to Dunbar personally.

scope.description: The 1860 transcription, supplied heading, marginal summaries, notes, and chronological placement

assertion.notes: The shared corpus vocabulary label is broader than this individual letter; CME00126 chiefly concerns allegiance, cross-border access, and political aid.

subjects.label: Anglo-Scottish relations, claims, geography, and military planning

summaries.value: George Dunbar, Earl of March, tells Henry IV that the Duke of Rothesay wronged his daughter by marrying another woman despite a sealed obligation. Dunbar offers service to Henry, asks for a trusted intermediary and a safe-conduct for one hundred knights, squires, and servants, explains their asserted kinship, and requests aid. He closes from Dunbar on 18 February; a Middle French endorsement addresses the king of England.

CME00126 encodes Hingeston's complete letter X from the heading in the lower half of printed p. 23 through the endorsement near the top of p. 25. It excludes the preceding letter and translation above it on p. 23 and letter XI beginning below it on p. 25. Hingeston's title, witness note, marginal summaries, cross-references, date gloss, and earl-identification note are represented as editorial apparatus rather than medieval letter text.

The XML has one letter DIV1, two HEAD elements, eight NOTE elements, eight P elements, and four PB elements. Seven P elements form the main letter and one P is the endorsement tailnote. The initial unnumbered PB is administrative; numbered PBs map printed pp. 23-25. The XML's Rymer footnote reads viii. 181, while the inspected 1860 source page reads viii. 131.

assertion.notes: An established editorial access title, not a medieval title written on the letter.

titles.value: The Earl of March to Henry IV
Letter from George Dunbar, Earl of March, to Henry IV (18 February 1400)

work.author: George Dunbar, Earl of March
work.displayTitle: The Earl of March to Henry IV
work.preferredTitle: The Earl of March to Henry IV

publication.embeddedTitle: THE EARL OF MARCH TO HENRY IV.

subjects.label: Anglo-Scottish relations, claims, geography, and military planning

work.summary: George Dunbar, Earl of March, tells Henry IV that the Duke of Rothesay wronged his daughter by marrying another woman despite a sealed obligation. Dunbar offers service to Henry, asks for a trusted intermediary and a safe-conduct for one hundred knights, squires, and servants, explains their asserted kinship, and requests aid. He closes from Dunbar on 18 February; a Middle French endorsement addresses the king of England.
